planting.note: Broadcast seed in late summer to early fall (6–4 weeks before first frost) in zones 6–10; in zone 5, sow earlier in late summer to allow sufficient establishment before hard frost. Inoculate seed with Rhizobium leguminosarum trifolii for maximum nitrogen fixation.

directions: Broadcast seed in late summer or early fall onto a firm, weed-free seedbed in full sun at 15–25 lbs per acre; crimson clover germinates rapidly in warm fall soil and overwinters as a low rosette, then bolts to produce spectacular, elongated, bright-crimson flower heads in spring that are exceptionally attractive to honeybees, bumble bees, and many species of native bees. Terminate by mowing or tillage in spring before or at early bloom to maximize the nitrogen credit to the following crop; the stand naturally winterkills above zone 6 and can be managed as a spring-terminated annual in zones 7–9. Fixes 75–200 lbs of atmospheric nitrogen per acre depending on stand density and termination timing, making it one of the highest nitrogen-fixing annual cover crops available. Do not allow seed set before incorporation or it will become a weedy reseeder in subsequent crops.

planting.note: Broadcast seed after last frost when soils have warmed to at least 60°F; berseem clover grows quickly and can reach first cutting in as little as 45 days. In zones 9–10, sow in fall or late winter for a warm-season winter annual.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 15–20 lbs per acre after last frost into a firm, moist seedbed in full sun; berseem clover is a fast-maturing annual clover originating from the Nile Valley and Mediterranean region and is widely grown as a high-quality warm-season cover crop and forage throughout the southern United States. It produces high-quality, high-protein biomass that can be cut multiple times (cutting regrowth encourages multiple harvests) and is considered superior forage to most other clovers for livestock and poultry because it does not cause bloat. Inoculate seed with Rhizobium leguminosarum trifolii for nitrogen fixation; fixes 80–150 lbs N/acre in a single season. Berseem clover is frost-sensitive and dies at 28°F, making termination easy in northern gardens by the first hard frost, with no need for mechanical destruction of the stand.

planting.note: Broadcast seed in early spring as soon as soils can be worked, or frost-seed in late winter; alsike clover is one of the few clovers that establishes well in wet, acidic, or poorly drained soils where red and white clover fail. Inoculate seed with Rhizobium leguminosarum trifolii.

directions: Broadcast seed at 6–8 lbs per acre in early spring in full sun onto a firm, moist seedbed; alsike clover tolerates wet, poorly drained, and moderately acidic soils (pH as low as 5.5) where red clover and white clover fail to persist, making it the preferred clover for heavy-clay, low-lying, or seasonally flooded areas. Trifolium hybridum produces erect to ascending stems with smooth trifoliate leaves and distinctive pink-and-white bicolor flower heads that are exceptionally attractive to honeybees and are considered one of the best bee plants among the clovers; it is a natural hybrid species named for its intermediate characteristics between red and white clover. It is shorter-lived than red clover in persistently dry soils but outperforms all other clovers on wet, cold, or acidic ground. Fixes 80–160 lbs N/acre and is widely used in cool, wet northern and Pacific Northwest cover crop mixes.

planting.note: Sow direct in early spring in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, chalky or limey soil in full sun; sainfoin requires good drainage and calcareous soil — it will not thrive on acid, waterlogged, or clay-heavy ground. Sow seed-in-pod (hulled or unhulled) at 25–40 lbs per acre.

directions: Sow direct in early spring at 25–40 lbs per acre (seed-in-pod) in well-drained, calcareous, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; sainfoin is a deep-rooted perennial legume from the Eurasian steppes and chalk grasslands that is exceptional for dry, poor, chalky, and limestone soils where most other legumes perform poorly. Onobrychis viciifolia produces upright, pinnate-leafed stems and striking spikes of vivid rose-pink, veined, pea-like flowers from late spring through summer that are among the most attractive of any cover crop and are magnets for bumblebees and honeybees; the name 'sainfoin' means 'healthy hay' in French, reflecting its long history as premium livestock forage. Unlike most legumes, sainfoin does not cause bloat in ruminants due to its condensed tannin content, making it particularly valuable as pasture in extensive livestock systems. Sainfoin fixes atmospheric nitrogen through Rhizobium symbiosis and the deep taproot mines subsoil nutrients, breaking up hardpan; it is very drought tolerant once established and thrives on thin, stony calcareous soils.

planting.note: Broadcast seed in early spring on a firm, weed-free seedbed; bird's-foot trefoil establishes slowly in its first season but becomes persistent and competitive once rooted. Inoculate seed with Rhizobium loti; do not cover seed more than 1/4 inch as seedlings are small and lack vigor to emerge from depth.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 4–6 lbs per acre in early spring on a firm, moist seedbed; bird's-foot trefoil is a creeping to semi-erect perennial legume that tolerates a wider range of difficult soil conditions than most clovers, including moderately acidic soils (pH 5.5–7.5), poorly drained or wet sites, and low-fertility soils, making it extremely useful for establishing cover and fixing nitrogen on disturbed, marginal, or problem sites. Lotus corniculatus produces cheerful clusters of yellow and orange pea flowers from late spring through fall that are excellent for pollinators, particularly bumblebees; the clusters of seed pods resemble a bird's foot or claw, giving rise to the common name. It is widely used in meadow mixes, roadsides, and erosion control seedings but can spread aggressively and become invasive in some native plant communities in North America — avoid planting near intact native grasslands or meadows. Does not cause bloat in livestock and fixes 50–100 lbs N/acre annually.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall for natural cold stratification over winter, or cold-stratify seed for 30–60 days and sow in early spring; seed requires scarification (nicking or hot-water soak) plus cold stratification to break dormancy. Best results come from fall sowing directly into a prepared seedbed or native meadow area.

directions: Sow scarified and cold-stratified seed in fall or early spring in full sun in well-drained to moderately moist, loamy to sandy soil; Canada milk vetch is a native North American perennial legume of prairies, open woodlands, stream banks, and disturbed areas from the Atlantic coast west to the Rocky Mountains, making it an excellent choice for prairie restoration, native meadow establishment, and pollinator habitat seedings across much of the country. Astragalus canadensis produces upright stems 2–4 feet tall clad in pinnately compound leaves, topped in summer with dense, cylindrical spikes of cream to greenish-white pea flowers that attract hummingbirds, bumblebees, and a variety of native bees; it spreads slowly by rhizomes and self-seeds to form colonies. As a native legume, it supports specialist native bee species that depend on Astragalus pollen and provides high-protein forage for deer and other wildlife; it fixes atmospheric nitrogen through Mesorhizobium symbiosis and is well adapted to the droughts and extreme temperature swings of prairie and plains environments. Requires patience — establish slowly in year one but become robust and competitive by year two.
